response:
The spring season brings many travelers to the city for their journeys. The fare $\eta$ depends directly on the random driving distance $\xi$ in this scenario. If the distance $\xi$ is less than or equal to 4 kilometers then the fare $\eta$ equals 10 yuan. When the distance $\xi$ exceeds 4 kilometers the fare $\eta$ becomes 10 plus 2 times the rounded up excess distance. This formula accounts for both the base rate and the extra charges per kilometer. The spring weather often influences how long taxis stop at traffic lights or for passengers.

A passenger paid 38 yuan which means the calculated distance was 19 kilometers based on the fare rule. Since the actual travel distance was 15 kilometers the extra 4 kilometers must come from stopping time. Each 5 minutes of stopping time converts to 1 kilometer of driving distance in this city system. Therefore the taxi stopped for at most 20 minutes to generate those additional 4 kilometers of charge. This calculation shows how waiting time directly increases the final cost for the rider. The spring breeze blows gently as the driver explains these rules to confused tourists.
